food_subgroup: Rice-based mixed meal set with protein accompaniments

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific entry for Nasi Lemak as a complete set meal. FOODON_00002809 (coconut rice food product) is the closest parent. The full set includes sambal, ikan bilis, peanuts, egg, chicken wing, and tempura fish which are not captured by this code.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKW (rice-based composite dish) is the closest defensible parent category. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for Nasi Lemak set meals with multiple protein accompaniments.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Singaporean mixed rice set meal.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Nasi Lemak set plate comprising approximately 200 g of coconut rice, one chicken wing (~60 g), one piece of tempura fish (~80 g), sambal chilli (~30 g), ikan bilis and peanuts (~20 g), and one egg (~60 g), totalling approximately 450 g.

nutrient_source.title: Nasi Lemak (with fried chicken wing and fried fish) — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo database contains entries for Nasi Lemak with fried chicken wing and Nasi Lemak with fried fish separately. Values here are adapted from the HPB composite Nasi Lemak set entry (with chicken wing and fried fish accompaniments) and cross-referenced with the HPB Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food publication. The tempura batter on the fish is treated as equivalent to a standard fried fish coating as no specific tempura fish entry exists in HPB. Per-100g values are derived from the HPB Nasi Lemak set meal data normalised to 100 g.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — White rice (cooked) and composite rice-based meals
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Nasi Lemak set with tempura fish and chicken wing. GI of 55 is estimated based on the GI of coconut rice (the dominant carbohydrate component), which is reported at approximately 54–56 in the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked white rice with coconut milk. According to the University of Sydney GI Database, a GI of 55 or less is classified as 'low'. The fat and protein content of the accompaniments (chicken wing, fish, egg, sambal, ikan bilis) further attenuate the glycemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 91.8) / 100 ≈ 50.5, where carbohydrate per 450 g serving = 20.4 × 4.5 = 91.8 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from sambal chilli, ikan bilis, and frying
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish as a single serving provides approximately 1,395 mg of sodium (310 mg per 100 g × 450 g), representing over 60% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg; portion reduction and requesting less sambal and ikan bilis can help mitigate sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load from coconut rice combined with high total caloric density
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should exercise caution with this meal as the estimated glycemic load per serving is approximately 50.5 (high), driven by the large portion of coconut rice, despite its low glycemic index; reducing the rice portion to half and pairing with the protein accompaniments can help moderate postprandial blood glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from coconut milk in rice and deep-fried chicken wing, combined with high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of this meal due to its significant saturated fat content (approximately 16.2 g per serving from coconut milk and deep-frying) and high sodium load, both of which are associated with elevated cardiovascular risk; occasional consumption in reduced portions is preferable to regular intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nasi Lemak Set with Tempura Fish and Chicken Wing is a popular Singaporean hawker-style set meal centred on fragrant pandan-infused coconut rice, which is the defining component of the dish. The rice is cooked with coconut milk and pandan leaves, giving it a rich, creamy flavour and a slightly sticky texture. The set is served with a signature sambal chilli (a spiced chilli paste that is mildly sweet and savoury), crispy deep-fried ikan bilis (dried anchovies), roasted peanuts, a hard-boiled or fried egg, a deep-fried chicken wing, and a piece of tempura-battered fried fish. The combination of textures — soft coconut rice, crunchy ikan bilis and peanuts, crispy battered fish, and juicy chicken wing — makes this a satisfying and complete meal. Nutritionally, this set is calorie-dense at approximately 195 kcal per 100 g, with a moderate protein content of 8.2 g, significant fat at 9.8 g (including 3.6 g saturated fat from coconut milk and frying), and carbohydrates at 20.4 g per 100 g. A full serving of approximately 450 g provides around 878 kcal. The sodium content is high at 310 mg per 100 g, making the full serving a significant source of dietary sodium. The glycemic index is estimated at 55 (low), but the glycemic load per serving is approximately 50.5 (high), driven primarily by the coconut rice portion size. Clinically, this meal is of concern for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease due to its high sodium, saturated fat, and glycemic load. It is a culturally iconic dish in Singapore, commonly consumed at breakfast, lunch, or dinner, and is widely available at hawker centres, food courts, and casual dining restaurants such as Qi Ji.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Nasi Lemak set with chicken wing and fish?
Qi Ji Nasi Lemak Set 4 calories
Nasi Lemak with tempura fish calories
Is Nasi Lemak high in saturated fat?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo database entries for Nasi Lemak with chicken wing and fried fish, which are the closest available approved-source matches. No single HPB entry exactly matches this specific set (tempura fish + chicken wing combination from Qi Ji). GI is an analogous estimate based on coconut rice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source database for this food category. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source database for this food category. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source database for this food category. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are estimated from HPB compositional data for similar fried rice-based set meals and are approximate. The serving size of 450 g is an estimate based on typical Nasi Lemak set meal weights reported in HPB portion guidance; actual restaurant portion may vary. The food_group has been reclassified from the input value of 'Meat and alternatives' to 'Mixed' as this is a composite set meal containing rice, protein, and condiment components. Halal status is assumed based on Qi Ji being a predominantly halal-certified chain in Singapore; consumers should verify with the specific outlet. No customisation of the set is available per the restaurant description.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected `gi_category` from 'medium' to 'low' as a GI of 55 is classified as low by the University of Sydney GI Database.
Updated the `gi_source.source_match_notes` to explicitly state the classification of GI 55 as 'low'.
Added the missing alias 'Nasi Lemak Set - Ikan Tempura & Sayap Ayam' from `aliases_by_language.romanized` to the flat `aliases` list to ensure consistency.
Updated the `guidance` for `type2_diabetes` to clarify the high glycemic load despite the low glycemic index.
Updated `veritas_meta.iteration_count_used` to 2 and `veritas_meta.generated_at` to current timestamp.
Updated `veritas_qc.criteria` notes for q4 and q8 to reflect the corrections.
